pages.raw_text:
A personal check for $4,000 ten
dered by M. j. Gordon. former
counsel for the Great Northern, as
a deposit in tax case brought by
the Hill road agaisnt Stevens
county, has fallen into the hands
of the grand jury.


The Stevens county officials
have learned that there is no
money behind the check, which
still stands as a legal tender ON de-
post with the treasurer of Stevens
county, while the case is on appeal
to the supreme court of the state.


1t is believed that the Great
Northern set aside the funds rep
resented by the deposit and the
claim will be made, as in other
financial transactions involving
Judge Gordon and the railroad,
that Gordon used the money for
other purposes connected with the
service of his employers. The fact
that check without funds to back
1t is on deposit In lawsuit that
requires tender of cash or its
equivalent remains to be eX-
planned, either by the Great North
ern or Gordon.


The grand jury wanted to get an
explanation from Judge f. V.
Brown, the new general western
counsel for the Great Northern,
and issued subpoena to Brown to
bring all the papers in the case to
court. Judge Brown refused to
comply with the order and a cita-
tion for contempt of court has
gone to Seattle to compel his at
tendance before Judge Huneke and
offer an explanation why she
should not be punished for con-
tempt of court.


An effort was then made yester-
day afternoon to compel A. j.
Laughon, in charge of the Great
Northern legal department in Spo-
kane, to furnish the papers and
correspondence in connection with
this Stevens county tax case, ana
he likewise refused and has been
cited to appear for contempt next
Monday.


The states Attorneys believe
that the Great Northern put up the
money involved in the $2S,000
Stevens county tender. that the
funds were used by Gordon. and
that he railroad, as in the Sparrow
case, will assume the burden of
the loss and shield Gordon.


To unquestionably establish this
condition Of things the state is
making every effort to bring evi
dence to light. Two representa-
tives Of the prosecuting attorneys
Office are out of the city today, and
when the final showdown comes
the state hopes to have the evi
dence that 1t seeks.


There were two tax cases in Ste
vens county, involving the Spokane
Falls 4 Northern branch OF the
Great Northern. In the case re
lating to the taxes of 1906 a de
post of $4,000 was put up while
the litigation was pending and the
matter was finally settled. Anoth
or suit, the one under investiga
tIon, related to the taxes for 1907
and was begun last year and Il
